concept:
Tested 4 ? Risk factors for DVT are:
Stasis
Endothelial injury
Hypercoagulability
Hampton's hump on an X-ray indicates: Pulmonary Embolism (PE)
Criteria for exudative effusion are:
Pleural/serum protein more than 0.5
Pleural/serum LDH more than 0.6
Causes of exudative effusion are: Think of leaky capillaries:
Malignancy
TB
Bacterial infection
Viral infection
Pulmonary Embolism (PE) with infarct
Pancreatitis
Causes of transudative effusion are: Think of intact capillaries:
CHF
Liver disease
Kidney disease
Protein losing enteropathy
Pulmonary lesion of sarcoidosis is: Noncaseating granulomas
Normalizing PCO2 in a patient having an asthma exacerbation may indicate: Fatigue and impending respiratory failure
Patient has:
Dyspnea
Lateral hilar lymphodenopathy on CXR
Increased ACE
Hypercalcemia indicating: Sarcoidosis
Treatment for child presenting with acute asthma attack: IV steroids
Curschmann's spirals (whorled mucous plugs) indicate: Bronchial Asthma
PFT showing decrease FEV1/FVC indicate: Obstructive pulmonary disease (e.g. asthma)
PFT showing increased FEV1/FVC indicate: Restrictive pulmonary disease
Infant presents with:
Failure to thrive
Frequent pulmonary infections
Greasy stools What is the diagnostic test? Sweat chloride test more than 60 mEq/L=CF (in children)
Common pathogens in CF patients are:
Pseudomonas
S. Aureus
Honeycomb pattern on CXR. Diagnosis? Treatment?
Diagnosis: Diffuse interstitial pulmonary fibrosis
Treatment:
Supportive care
Steroids may help
Treatment for superior vena cava syndrome is: Radiation
Treatment for mild, persistent asthma is: Inhaled Beta-agonists and inhaled corticosteroids
What are the blood gas values for a pulmonary embolism:
Hypoxia
Hypocarbia
Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) associated with hypercalcemia is: Squamous Cell Carcinmoa (SCC)
Lung cancer assciated with SIADH is: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC)
Lung cancer highly related to cigarette exposure is: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC)
Tall, white male presents with acute shortness of breath. Diagnosis? Treatment?
Diagnosis: Spontaneous pneumothorax
Treatment:
Spontaneous regression
Oxygen may be helpful
Treatment of tension pneumothorax is: Immediate needle thoracostomy
Characteristics favoring carcinoma in an isolated pulmonary nodule:
Patient more than 45 to 50 years old
Lesions new or larger in comparison to old films
Absence of calcification or irregular calcification
Size more than 2cm
Irregular margins
Hypoxemia and pulmonary edema with normal pulmonary capillary wedge pressure indicate: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)
Silicosis increases the risk of what infection: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Causes of hypoxemia are:
Right to left shunt
Hypoventilation
Low inspired oxygen tension
Diffusion defect
Ventilation-perfusion mismatch
Classic CXR findings for pulmonary edema are:
Cardiomegaly
Prominant pulmonary vessels
Kerley B lines
"Bat's-wing" appearance of hilar shadows
Perivascular cuffing
Peribronchial cuffing
